summary: Gruber reports on his experience with SpamAssassin after his web host added support for it, finding it catches 90% of spam on its own and 97% when combined with his personal Mailsmith filters. He had been skeptical, expecting that smart spammers would simply test their messages against the open-source tool to evade detection. However, he found that 'smart spammers' are rare and SpamAssassin's frequently updated ruleset stays ahead of most countermeasures. Despite the strong results, he warns that a 97% detection rate becomes inadequate as spam volume scales from hundreds to thousands per week.
key_points: SpamAssassin alone catches 90% of spam; combined with personal filters, the detection rate reaches 97%
The open-source nature of SpamAssassin theoretically lets spammers test and evade it, but smart spammers are apparently rare
SpamAssassin's constantly updated ruleset helps it stay ahead of spammer countermeasures
A 97% detection rate is fine at 500 spams per week but falls apart at 5,000 — the direction things are heading
money_quotes: As I type these words, I've received exactly 450 spams in the last 7 days.
A smart spammer, quite obviously, would do well to test their messages by sending them through SpamAssassin, then tweaking their content until they slip through undetected.
"Smart spammers", apparently, are a very rare breed.
My concern, however, is that "very good" is not good enough.
97 percent is a fine detection rate when you receive 500 spams a week. It's not so good if you receive 5,000 spams a week — and that's where it seems we're heading.
I do get a few false positives, but they're almost always borderline spam anyway — marketing messages from online retailers, or five-times forwarded "jokes" from distant relatives who use AOL.
key_insight: Even a 97% spam detection rate becomes inadequate when spam volume grows exponentially, making 'very good' filtering fundamentally insufficient against a scaling problem.
